actions.thought: Being on the button in a 3-handed game provides a significant positional advantage. K4 suited is a perfectly acceptable hand to open with in this spot to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. I will raise to 2.5 times the big blind to initiate the betting and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or define the ranges of my opponents.

In 3-handed play, the Button opens with a wide range. 7h8h is a strong suited connector that plays very well as a 3-bet from the Small Blind. By 3-betting, we take the initiative, put pressure on the Button's wide opening range, and discourage the Big Blind from squeezing. If called, we have excellent playability post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack suited. The Button opened to 2.5x, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 8.5x. KJs is a strong hand in 3-handed play, but calling a 3-bet out of position against a 'Pro' requires caution. However, the pot odds are roughly 2.6:1, and KJs has good playability and nut potential (flushes, straights). I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as folding this early with a premium-adjacent hand in a 3-max game might be too tight, though I must be wary of being dominated by hands like AK, KQ, or AJ.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind and a cold-call from the big blind, I am getting decent pot odds to see a flop in position. While K4s is not a premium hand, calling the additional 600 chips (to make my total investment 850) is mathematically sound given my positional advantage. A 4-bet bluff here would be high variance and potentially unnecessary against two players, so calling to see a flop is the most controlled and balanced strategic decision.

We hit bottom pair with no spade on a heavily connected monotone board (Ks 9s 8s) in a 3-way 3-bet pot. Both opponents have reasonably strong ranges since they called my 3-bet pre-flop. Out of position without a flush draw, our hand has very poor equity and playability against continuing ranges. Betting here would be suicidal as we have no blockers to the nuts and little chance to improve. Checking with the intention to fold to any action is the safest and most standard play.

I have top pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker on a very coordinated monotone board (three spades). Since I do not hold a spade, my hand is vulnerable to any spade in my opponents' ranges. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. In a three-way pot on this type of board, checking is the standard play with a hand like mine to control the pot size and see how the Button reacts. I can evaluate my position further on the turn.

The board is extremely wet with three spades, making my Top Pair with a weak kicker (Kc 4c) very vulnerable. Since both opponents checked, I have the positional advantage to take a free card and re-evaluate on the turn. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws, so checking is the most cautious and profitable path to control the pot size.

The board now has four spades (Ks 9s 8s Ts) and I hold 7h8h, giving me no flush draw and only a weak pair. My straight draw is irrelevant since any flush beats it. Against two opponents in a 3-way 3-bet pot, it is extremely likely at least one of them holds a spade. I have essentially zero equity and no credible way to bluff. Checking and preparing to fold to any bet is the only viable option.

The board now shows four spades (Ks 9s 8s Ts), and I do not hold a spade in my hand. My top pair of Kings is extremely vulnerable as any single spade in either opponent's hand now makes a flush. Pro, the pre-flop 3-better, has checked for the second time. Given the 4-flush board, betting without a spade is highly risky and unlikely to fold out any better hands while only getting called by hands that beat me. Checking is the most prudent action to see a river and potentially reach a cheap showdown or fold to significant pressure.

The board has become extremely dangerous with four spades present. My pair of Kings, without a spade, is merely a bluff-catcher and is likely beaten by any single spade in my opponents' holdings. Since both opponents have checked twice, betting is unlikely to yield value from worse hands and only puts me at risk of being check-raised or value-bet into. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and reach a showdown cheaply, maintaining control of the pot in a high-variance spot.

On the river, the board pairs the ten. I have 7h8h, making tens and eights, but there are four spades on the board and any higher pair beats me. Against two opponents who called a 3-bet pre-flop, someone likely has a better pair, a ten, or a spade. Since I have no blockers to the flush or full house, bluffing into two players is very likely to fail. Checking and surrendering the pot is the safest and most optimal play.

The board has four spades and I do not hold any. While I have top pair (Kings and Tens on the river), any single spade in an opponent's hand makes a flush. Everyone has checked the flop and turn, and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked the river. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking is the most logical play to reach a showdown and hope my two pair is good against non-spade hands.

The board is extremely dangerous with four spades and a pair of Tens. My pair of Kings is essentially a bluff-catcher at this point. Since both opponents have checked the flop, turn, and river, it is highly likely that nobody holds a strong hand or they are all trapping, but betting into this board only isolates me against better holdings that have me crushed. There is no value in betting because I will likely only be called by hands that beat me, and checking behind allows me to reach showdown to see if my pair of Kings holds up against two opponents who have also shown weakness throughout the hand.

On the Button in a 3-handed game, pocket sevens is a strong starting hand. An open raise to 2.5x the big blind is standard to either steal the blinds or take control of the pot going into the flop with positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four offsuit (K4o). The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. K4o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a standard button opening range. Defending the Small Blind with such a wide, non-connected, and dominated hand is generally a negative expected value (-EV) play. Since I have no specific reads on Pro's opening frequencies yet and I want to maintain my chip lead, I will make the disciplined fold.

I am in the big blind with one of the weakest possible starting hands (2c, 6d). While the pot odds technically suggest a call, playing a hand this poor out of position against an aggressive button raiser is negative expected value. It is best to fold, preserve my stack, and wait for a more playable hand given we are only in the second hand of the tournament.
Holding Queen-Trey offsuit on the button, this hand is too weak to justify a raise or a limp even in a three-handed game. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

T4o is a very weak starting hand with low playability post-flop. Even with the discounted price to complete the small blind, there is little strategic value in continuing with this hand against the big blind. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
Ace-Nine offsuit is a solid opening hand in a three-handed game on the button. I will raise to 250 to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds.
42o is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button while out of position in the Small Blind makes this an easy and trivial fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Five offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. With 150 more to call into a pot of 400, I am getting approximately 2.6:1 odds. A5o is a standard defending hand against a Button opening range. I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as 3-betting might be too aggressive against this specific player without more information.

I am first to act on a flop of Ks 8d Th after calling from the Big Blind pre-flop. This board favors the Button's opening range, as it contains several high cards and straight possibilities (QJ, J9). My Ace-Five offsuit has very little equity here beyond a backdoor straight draw and one overcard (the Ace). The standard play in this situation is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react.

I have Ace-high on a board of King-Ten-Eight. My hand is currently weak and does not have significant equity against a defending big blind range that likely includes Kings, Tens, or even Eights. Betting here as a continuation bet would purely be a bluff, but since I have an Ace, I have some potential showdown value if the hand checks down. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity for free, as I am unlikely to get folds from better hands that have connected with this board.

On this turn (Js), the board has become extremely coordinated (Ks 8d Th Js). I currently only hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked back on the flop, which could indicate a marginal made hand or a draw. Given that this board texture heavily favors many hands in a Button's opening range, I will check again to see if Flashlight checks behind once more, allowing me a free river to potentially hit my straight or a miracle Ace.

The board is highly coordinated with K-J-T-8, and the opponent has checked twice, suggesting they may have a marginal hand or a draw. My Ace-high hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many pairs. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity from the gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) for free, and avoid bloating the pot against a range that could contain sets or straights.

The board is highly coordinated with multiple straight possibilities (any Q or 9 makes a straight). The pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, has checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates either a weak made hand or a complete miss. While my Ace-high has very little showdown value, checking is the most prudent play. I'll hope for a check-back from Flashlight to reach showdown for free; if they bet, my hand is likely not strong enough to call given the number of pairs and straights possible on this board.

The board texture is K-J-T-8-3, which connects well with most ranges, especially considering the opponent checked through all three streets. My Ace-high (Ah 9d) has showdown value against missed draws or lower-card hands, but it is effectively a bluff-catcher. Betting here does not provide value, as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would be folding out hands I currently beat or forcing a raise from hands that dominate me. Checking back is the optimal way to realize my showdown equity.

I am on the Button (acting first 3-handed) with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a mediocre, disconnected hand. While I have positional advantage post-flop, raising J9o here isn't highly profitable unless the blinds are extremely tight. Given the early tournament stages and full stacks, the most disciplined and standard action is to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7s and it has been folded to me. This is a standard situation to open-raise in a short-handed game to either win the blinds immediately or play a heads-up pot with a hand that has decent post-flop playability. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard size to apply pressure on the Big Blind.

The opponent opened from the small blind to 300, which is a standard raise. With Ace-six offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to defend my big blind, as I will have position throughout the hand post-flop. Folding would be too conservative, and while 3-betting is an option, calling is the most balanced and strategic play here to keep the pot manageable while realizing my equity.

I raised pre-flop from the Small Blind and am now facing the Big Blind on an Ace-high board (3c Td Ah). This is a classic spot for a continuation bet. Even though I only have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw, the Ace is much better for my perceived range than for Flashlight's. A small bet of 200 (1/3 of the pot) should be effective at getting him to fold hands that missed the flop, such as small pairs or random cards.

I hold top pair with an Ace, which is a strong hand on this dry board of Ace-Ten-Three. My opponent's bet of 200 into an 800 pot is quite small, suggesting they might be continuation betting with a marginal hand or a draw. Since I have top pair, folding is not an option, and raising would likely only isolate me against hands that have me beaten or fold out the hands I can get value from. Therefore, calling is the most strategic approach to control the pot size and see the turn card.

The Jack of Diamonds on the turn is a favorable card for my hand as it provides me with a Queen-high flush draw and a backdoor straight draw. Having been the pre-flop aggressor and having fired a continuation bet on the flop, I will continue my aggression with a second barrel. This semi-bluff puts maximum pressure on Flashlight's marginal hands like a Ten or a weak Jack, while I retain significant equity to improve to a flush on the river if called. A bet of 600 (60% of the pot) is a strong sizing that maintains the story of a powerful hand.

I hold top pair with an Ace on a board that has become slightly more coordinated with the turn Jack. My opponent's bet of 600 into a pot of 1000 is a significant sizing increase from the flop, indicating they likely have a piece of this board, such as a Ten, a Jack, or a draw. However, folding top pair here with decent pot odds would be an over-fold. I am currently holding a bluff-catcher or a hand with showdown value. Calling is the most appropriate line to keep the pot manageable and see the river, while avoiding turning my hand into a bluff by raising.

I have been the aggressor throughout this hand, and while I missed my flush and straight draws, the river pairing the Ace is a very strong card for my perceived range. Having raised pre-flop and bet both the flop and turn, a third barrel on this board texture puts significant pressure on Flashlight's marginal holdings, such as a Ten or a Jack. Since I have no showdown value with Queen-high, I must continue the bluff to win the pot. A bet of 1200 into the 2200 pot (roughly 55%) represents a strong hand like a trip Aces or a full house, and it is statistically unlikely that Flashlight holds the remaining Ace.

On the river, the board has paired with the Ace of Clubs, giving me Trip Aces (As, Ah, Ac). While this is a very strong hand, the board is highly coordinated with potential straights (KQ) and full houses (TT, JJ, 33). Raising here would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten or result in folding out weaker hands that might have been bluffing. Calling is the most balanced and secure play, allowing me to capture value from any bluffs or inferior value hands while minimizing risk against the top of the opponent's range.
I have pocket nines on the button in a 3-handed game, which is a strong opening hand. I will raise to 300 to build value and take control of the pot with position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-7 offsuit, which is a weak hand to defend from out of position against an aggressive button open. Given the depth of play and my position, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid playing a bloated pot with a mediocre holding.

Flash is raising from the Button, which is typically a very wide range. Holding AJo in the Big Blind, we are well ahead of this range. 3-betting is the optimal play here to extract value, deny equity, and take the initiative heading into the flop out of position.

I have pocket nines, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I opened from the button, and the big blind (Pro) 3-bet me. Given the stack depths and my position, calling is the most standard and effective play. It allows me to see a flop and realize my equity while maintaining the advantage of acting last on all subsequent streets. Folding is too conservative, and 4-betting might lead to an over-commitment before seeing the board.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, this 6h 4c 8h flop completely misses my Ad Jc and connects heavily with the caller's range, which includes medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, and flush draws. Being out of position with just Ace-high and no significant draw, the standard play is to check. C-betting here would often result in getting called or raised by better hands, and I have very low equity against a continuing range. Checking preserves my stack and allows me to re-evaluate based on my opponent's action.

I have an overpair with my pocket nines on a relatively low board of 6h 4c 8h. After calling a 3-bet pre-flop, the aggressor (Pro) has checked to me. This check could represent weakness (missing with overcards like AK or AQ) or a trap with a larger overpair. I should bet for value to get called by draws (like heart flush draws or straight draws) and smaller pairs, while also protecting my hand against overcards that could fall on the turn. A bet of 1000 into a pot of 2150 is a standard sizing to achieve these goals.

The flop completely missed our AdJc, and we have no flush draw or meaningful straight draw. The 6h-4c-8h board heavily favors our opponent's pre-flop calling range, which contains many suited connectors, medium pocket pairs, and flush draws. Facing a bet of nearly half the pot out of position with just Ace-high, continuing would be unprofitable. The best course of action is to fold and preserve our stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Given my position, I have the advantage to dictate the pace of the hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot with initiative.

With pocket eights in the Small Blind facing a Button open, I have a strong hand that can be played aggressively. 3-betting here denies equity to the Big Blind, takes the betting initiative, and extracts value or wins the pot outright pre-flop against a wider Button opening range. I will size up to roughly 3.3x the open to push out marginal hands.

I am in the big blind holding 2s 4h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Flashlight opened from the button and Pro 3-bet from the small blind. Given the strength of my hand and the action ahead of me, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I opened with Queen-Jack offsuit from the button, which is a strong range for 3-handed play. After being 3-bet by the small blind, calling is the most balanced and highest EV play given I have position for the rest of the hand. 4-betting with QJo would likely isolate myself against a stronger range and turn my hand into a bluff, whereas calling allows me to realize my equity and navigate the post-flop streets with a positional advantage.

The board is A-K-3 rainbow, which hits the 3-bet calling range of the Button extremely well (lots of Ax, Kx, and Broadway hands). With pocket 8s, I have almost no value and bluffing here will rarely succeed against top pair or second pair. Checking to evaluate the opponent's action is the safest and most standard play. I plan to fold if faced with a significant bet.

The flop of Ace-King-Three is extremely strong for the Small Blind's 3-betting range. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, as betting would likely only be called by better hands (Aces and Kings) that have me dominated, or check-raised, forcing me to fold. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially catch a Ten on the turn to make a straight.

The turn brings a 7d, not changing the board texture much. The board is still dangerous with an Ace and a King. Flashlight checking back the flop could indicate a weak Ace, a King controlling the pot, a middle pocket pair like QQ-99, or just missed broadways. I still have showdown value against their missed broadways, but betting here turns my hand into a bluff and risks getting called by better hands. Checking maintains pot control and aims to get to a cheap showdown.

The board of Ace-King-Three-Seven is highly coordinated with the likely 3-betting range of the Small Blind (Pro), who has shown no aggression after the pre-flop raise. My hand, Queen-Jack high, has minimal showdown value but functions as a gutshot straight draw. Betting here is unlikely to fold out an Ace or King, and checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity for free, hoping to hit a Ten on the river while avoiding a check-raise that would force me to fold.

The river brings an 8, giving me a perfectly disguised set of eights. Given that Flashlight checked both the flop and the turn, their range is heavily weighted toward marginal showdown value, such as middle pocket pairs (QQ-99), a weak King, or a cautious Ace. If I check, they are very likely to check behind with those types of hands. Therefore, I must bet to extract value. Betting around half-pot gives them an attractive price to bluff-catch with their marginal hands while still building the pot significantly.

I hold Queen-high with no pair on an Ace-King high board. My hand has no showdown value, and calling a river bet against an opponent who 3-bet pre-flop is extremely risky. While the opponent's check on the flop and turn could indicate a missed draw, they likely have at least a King or a better high-card hand to value bet on the river. With no pair to beat even a modest bluff, calling is a negative EV play. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Being first to act on the Button 3-handed with KQ offsuit, this is a premium starting hand and a mandatory open. A raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is a standard size to steal the blinds or build the pot for value when called.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 8c, a strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5bb. Calling is the most standard play here to see a flop and realize the equity of a suited connector without bloating the pot out of position against an aggressive player. Folding is too conservative, and 3-betting is an option but calling preserves the ability to play a disguised hand on many board textures.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six suited, a playable hand that merits defending against a button raise given the pot odds. I need to call 150 to contest a pot of 600. Since the Small Blind has already called, I am closing the action pre-flop. Raising here could be too aggressive with this specific holding against two opponents, so a call is the most balanced and profitable play to see a flop.

I am first to act on a flop of Kd-Ts-4c with 9c-8c. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro). Leading into the aggressor (donk betting) is rarely correct here, especially with only backdoor draws. Checking is the standard play to see how the action develops.

I have top pair, King-high with a weak 6-kicker on a relatively dry board. Leading into two opponents with this hand is ill-advised as it bloats the pot without guaranteeing I have the best hand given the potential for better kickers in their ranges. Checking is the standard play to maintain pot control and proceed to the turn with showdown value.

With top pair and a strong kicker (KQ) on a Kd-Ts-4c rainbow board, I should bet for value and protection. The pot is 750, and both opponents have checked to me. A bet of around 40% of the pot (300 chips) targets worse kings, strong tens, and straight draws (like QJ, J9).

I have 9-high with virtually no equity on a King-high board that favors the pre-flop aggressor. Pro has bet 300 into the pot after I checked. With only runner-runner draws and no pair, there is no justification for calling or raising in this spot, especially being out of position.

I have top pair, King-high, on a dry, rainbow board. While my kicker is weak, top pair is a strong hand in this spot, especially considering Pro is on the button and likely continuation betting a wide range. Folding would be too conservative given the pot odds of 3.5:1, and raising might force out hands I beat or isolate myself against a stronger King. Calling is the most disciplined and profitable play to evaluate the turn.

The turn card, the Jack of Spades, significantly coordinates the board, making it dangerous for my one-pair hand. While I have top pair (Kings) and a gutshot straight draw to the Queen, my opponent's betting range on the flop suggests they could have me beat or be holding a strong draw. Leading into them would likely only get called by hands that have me beat or isolate me against a strong range, while checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river. Checking is the most balanced and strategic decision.

The Js on the turn is a very connected card that improves many hands in the Big Blind's flop calling range, such as KJ, JT, QJ, and J9. While I have top pair and picked up an open-ended straight draw, betting now risks isolating myself against two pairs, straights (like AQ), or strong combo draws, while folding out weaker hands. Checking behind exercises pot control, guarantees I see the river to potentially hit my straight, and keeps the pot manageable.

The board is paired with Jacks and contains a King, Ten, and Four. My hand (King-Six) gives me two pair (Kings and Jacks), but it is very vulnerable given the board texture. With two Jacks on the board, any Jack beats me, as does any King with a better kicker or a straight. Since the turn was checked around, the pot size is 1350. Betting into my opponents here is not advisable as I will likely only be called by better hands, and checking allows me to control the pot size and proceed to showdown or fold if facing a significant bet.

On the river, the board pairs the Jack. My hand is Kings and Jacks with a Queen kicker. While I beat any Ten and worse Kings, a lot of hands in the Big Blind's flop-calling range beat me now, such as AK, KJ, QJ, JT, or J9. If I bet, I'm mostly getting called by better hands (like trip Jacks or a better kicker King) and folding out weaker hands or missed straight draws (like AQ). Checking behind ensures we get to showdown safely and avoids getting check-raised or value-owning ourselves.

I am on the button with QTs, a very strong starting hand for this position. Raising is the standard play here to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with standard opening sizes and allows me to play a pot in position against the blinds if they call.

Flash opened from the button. I am in the small blind with Ace-Three offsuit. While not a premium hand, it has reasonable equity in a three-handed game against a wide button opening range. Calling the 250 chips (for a total of 300 chips) is the most prudent play to see a flop while maintaining a manageable pot size, acknowledging that playing out of position is a disadvantage.

I have J9o in the Big Blind. The Button has opened to 300 (3bb) and the Small Blind has called. J9 offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially in a multiway pot. While the pot odds are decent (200 to call for a pot of 700), the poor playability and lack of suitedness of this hand make folding the most sensible action.

The board is 6h-4h-8d. My hand, Ad-3c, has no made pair and no flush draw. It is essentially Ace-high with very little showdown value. As the small blind out of position, checking is the standard play. I should not lead into the aggressor here with a weak hand that has limited equity against their opening range. I will check to see what the Button does.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and hold position against the Small Blind. The flop of 6h-4h-8d is somewhat coordinated, but I still maintain a range advantage with overpairs and high cards. Since Flashlight checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to take down the pot now or to maintain initiative. I'm betting 400, which is slightly more than half the pot.

The flop is 6h-4h-8d. I hold Ad-3c, which is essentially air with no made pair, no flush draw, and minimal straight equity. My opponent, Flash, has continuation-bet on a board that hits their button opening range quite well. Given that I am out of position with no meaningful equity and the pot odds don't justify a call, folding is the disciplined, professional play to preserve my stack.

I am holding 3d 9h, which is a trash hand with no significant connectivity or high-card value. It is mathematically unprofitable to call the 100 to play this hand from the button. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

J6o is a weak offsuit hand, and playing it from the small blind puts me out of position against the big blind post-flop. It's not strong enough to complete or attempt a steal against a competent opponent, so folding is the standard and safest play.

2s 8s is a very weak and disconnected hand, even when suited. Despite having the button advantage, this hand lacks the equity and playability required to open-raise or call against the blinds. Folding is the only optimal decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to play when folded to me. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play with a range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with low playability. Flash has raised from the Small Blind. Given the poor equity of my hand and the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand, defending is not profitable. Folding is the disciplined choice to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Trey offsuit. While 3-handed play warrants a wider opening range, Q3o is significantly below the threshold of hands that are profitable to raise from this position, especially against players who have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

With Flash (Button) having folded, I am in the Small Blind against Pro (Big Blind). Holding 9-8 offsuit, I am getting 2:1 pot odds (50 to call into a 150 pot) to see a flop. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, completing the blind is a standard, low-variance approach to see a flop heads-up with this type of holding.

With pocket sixes in the Big Blind against a Small Blind open limp, I have a strong enough hand to raise for value and protection. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop against weak limps, and punishes the limper while we will have position post-flop.

Holding 9-8 offsuit in the small blind against a raise from the big blind is unfavorable. Being out of position with a marginal hand makes it difficult to navigate post-flop play effectively. Given the tournament stage and position, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am dealt Queen-Four offsuit on the button. This is a very weak starting hand that does not warrant a raise or a call against two opponents. The most disciplined and profitable play in this spot is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

With King-Eight offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I have a decent hand to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind and frequently wins the pot uncontested.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q2o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I have position for the rest of the hand, Q2o is a very weak starting hand and does not play well post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the 3x raise size, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.
J2 offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely low playability and equity. Even from the Button, it is not worth attempting a steal. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

65o is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind out of position against the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal by raising or complete the bet, folding is a disciplined play to minimize risk with a marginal hand this early in the tournament, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if the Big Blind calls.

I am on the button with 86 suited, which is a strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game. Raising to 300 is a standard play to take control of the pot and utilize my positional advantage against the blinds. If I am re-raised, I will re-evaluate, but 86s plays well post-flop if called.

With Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind against a Button open, I have a strong enough hand to three-bet. Flash has shown aggression in previous hands, and three-betting allows me to take the initiative, neutralize the positional disadvantage, and define their range early. A raise to 950 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.
Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, A9 offsuit is far too weak to continue. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve my stack.

I am facing a 3-bet from the small blind after my button open. With 8s6s, I have a hand that plays very well post-flop, and I will have the benefit of position for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds (calling 650 to win a total pot of 2000) require roughly 32.5% equity, which this hand should comfortably have against a standard 3-betting range in a 3-handed game. I prefer calling over 4-betting to keep the pot manageable and utilize my positional advantage to realize my equity.

I hold top pair with top kicker (Ah, Qd) on a relatively safe board (Qc, 4d, 7c). Having 3-bet pre-flop, I should continue to show strength and extract value from hands like middle pairs, weaker queens, or underpairs. A bet of 700 into the 2000 pot is a standard continuation bet size that denies equity to potential draws and builds the pot for value.

I have a gutshot straight draw with my 8s6s on a board of Qc-4d-7c (needing a 5 for a straight). Flashlight has led for 700 into a 2000 pot, which is a standard 1/3 pot continuation bet after 3-betting pre-flop. The price to call is approximately 20.5% (700 / 3400), and while my direct equity with the gutshot is about 8.5% for the next card, the implied odds of hitting a 'hidden' straight in a 3-bet pot are significant. Additionally, I have the benefit of position, which may allow me to take the pot away on the turn if Flashlight checks. Folding now would be too tight given the odds and positional advantage.

I hold top pair with a top kicker on a board that is moderately coordinated with a flush draw. Given that I have the initiative from my pre-flop three-bet and subsequent flop continuation bet, checking now would likely be a sign of weakness and invite my opponent to take a free card or bluff me off the hand. I need to continue charging for potential draws (like the club draw) and extracting value from hands like middle pairs or weaker queens (e.g., QJ, QT). A bet of 1600, which is roughly half the pot, is appropriate to maintain control and build value.

Flashlight has followed up their pre-flop 3-bet and flop c-bet with a strong second barrel of 1600 into a 3400 pot. My 8s6s currently only has a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 for a straight), which gives me roughly a 9% chance to hit on the river. The pot odds offered are approximately 24% (1600 to win a total pot of 6600), which means the direct equity does not justify a call. While I have position, Flashlight's betting line represents significant strength (likely AA, KK, AQ, or perhaps a set of Tens), and the implied odds are not high enough to compensate for the lack of direct equity, especially as Flashlight is becoming pot-committed. Folding is the correct move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sevens (7c, 7s). In a three-handed game, 77 is a strong hand that plays well in position. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative, potentially thin the field, and maintain control of the pot. Given my position, this is a standard open-raise to extract value or take down the blinds.
I am dealt 2d 4c in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak, unplayable hand. Facing a raise from the Button, folding is the only viable option to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), 82o is simply too weak to defend profitably against a standard Button opening range. Saving chips for better spots is the more prudent play in a long-term tournament setting.
4h 7d is one of the weakest off-suit starting hands in Texas Hold'em. From the Button, there is no value in attempting to steal the blinds or play this hand. Folding is the only viable option.

Holding 7-6 offsuit in the small blind after the button folds. While this hand has some potential in a head-up situation, I am out of position against the chip leader, Flashlight. Given my current stack is the shortest, I prefer to play a tighter range and avoid unnecessary risks in blind-versus-blind confrontations with marginal holdings.

I am on the button with K9s, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. Following my established pattern of raising to 300 from this position, I will open the pot to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand with a suited King.

Qc5s is a weak holding, particularly out of position in the small blind against an opening range from the button. Calling would be speculative and likely unprofitable given the disadvantage of acting first throughout the post-flop streets, and there is still a player in the big blind. A fold is the most disciplined action.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while out of position. A3o is a weak, easily dominated hand that doesn't play well post-flop. Given that Flash has shown a willingness to call 3-bets previously, a 3-bet bluff is risky, and flatting is unprofitable. Folding is the safest and most standard option here.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game, especially in position on the Button. The standard strategy is to raise to gain initiative and potentially take down the blinds or play a larger pot with a superior hand. I will open to 300 chips.

K9o is too weak to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Being out of position with an easily dominated hand (by stronger Kings or better kickers) makes calling or 3-betting unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the most standard and solid choice here.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey suited (Js 3s). The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, J3s is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially against a 3x open. Although it is suited, the kicker is poor and it lacks connectivity. Given that Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands and I will be playing the rest of the hand out of position, I will choose to fold this weak holding and wait for better spots to commit chips.

KQ offsuit is a strong starting hand, especially when opening from the button. By raising, I can steal the blinds or take control of the pot going into the flop if called. A standard open of 2.5x the big blind is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind holding 8-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Even though it is a three-handed game and ranges are wider, 8-5 offsuit does not play well out of position and has very poor equity against a standard Button opening range. I should preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 5c, which is a very weak, disconnected hand. Facing a raise from the button, the pot odds do not justify calling with such low-equity cards. It is disciplined to fold here and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Ten-Four suited. While the button should open a wide range in 3-handed play, Ten-Four suited is near the bottom of that range. Given that both opponents have shown a willingness to 3-bet or defend their blinds aggressively in previous hands, I will opt for a conservative fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

The button has folded, leaving me in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. While King-five offsuit is not a premium holding, it is a perfectly reasonable hand to open-raise with from the small blind to attempt a steal of the blinds, given the positional advantage. I will raise to 300 to test the big blind's commitment to the pot.

I'm in the Big Blind with 6d 2d, which is an extremely weak hand with very little equity or playability, even suited. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, the correct play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand (4s 6c). Playing this hand in an unraised pot 3-handed is strategically unsound as it lacks strength and potential. The best move is to fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind with J9s, which is a strong and versatile hand in blind-vs-blind situations. Raising here puts pressure on the Big Blind, allows me to potentially win the 150 chips uncontested, and builds the pot with a hand that has great playability post-flop if called. A standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300. While ranges in blind-versus-blind battles are typically wider, Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand that lacks connectivity and high-card value. It will be difficult to play profitably post-flop even with closing the action. Given the strength of the hand and the standard 3x raise from Pro, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
I am on the Button with Q7 offsuit. This is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop and lacks the equity to justify an open raise. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, King-Trey offsuit is a marginal hand. While the price to call is small (only 50 more chips), I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against Flashlight, who has been active and has a larger stack. Given the early stage of the tournament and my position as the current short stack, I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for a more advantageous spot rather than playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak kicker.

T7o is a marginal hand that sits outside the standard opening range for the button in a 3-handed game. Given that both Flashlight and Pro have demonstrated aggressive tendencies and willingness to 3-bet or defend their blinds in previous hands, opening with such a weak hand is likely to lead to negative expected value. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind position against the Big Blind after the Button folded. With King-high, I have a reasonable hand for a heads-up situation. Raising to 300 is a standard move to attempt to steal the blinds and take initiative in the hand, putting pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 8d, which is a very weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. The Small Blind has raised to 3 BBs. I do not have sufficient equity or playability to defend with this hand, so folding is the optimal and standard decision.

I am dealt a very weak hand (3-5 offsuit) in a three-handed game. Even being on the button, this hand has extremely low equity and poor playability. It is standard to fold junk hands like this, regardless of position, to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o, a very weak unsuited hand. The Button has folded, making it head-to-head against the Big Blind. However, playing such a weak hand out of position is negative expected value. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am on the Button with a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand (Queen-Three offsuit). Stealing from the button with this hand is too risky and generally unprofitable, so the best decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four suited. The action has folded to me. While it is only 50 more to call to see a flop, Jack-Four is quite weak and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against Flashlight, who has a larger stack and has shown a willingness to be aggressive. Folding here is a conservative play that preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I have 8c 7c on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a strong speculative hand suited for opening. I will put in a standard 3x open raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play a post-flop pot with position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine suited. The button (Flash) has raised to 300, and I have already posted 50 chips. While playing out of position post-flop is a disadvantage, King-Nine suited is a strong enough holding to defend against a standard button open, especially with healthy stack depth. 3-betting is an option, but calling allows me to see a flop with a decent hand and keep the pot size manageable, given that the Big Blind is still to act.

Q7o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, this hand performs very poorly in a multiway pot. Despite getting decent pot odds, our hand has little playability post-flop and high reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack.

I have middle pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. As the out-of-position player who called pre-flop, checking is the standard play here. This allows me to control the pot size and see how the button (the pre-flop aggressor) proceeds. My hand has decent showdown value, but I don't want to inflate the pot by betting into a range that likely hit this board well or is c-betting frequently.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 8c 7c on a Ts-2h-9c board. As the pre-flop aggressor on the button, I will continuation-bet this flop. This allows me to take down the pot immediately if Flashlight has missed, or build a larger pot for the times I hit my straight. A bet of 400 into a 700-chip pot (approx 57%) is a standard sizing for this texture.

I have middle pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board (T-9-2). A 400-chip continuation bet into a 700-chip pot is standard, and my hand has sufficient showdown value to continue. Folding would be overly tight, and raising is ill-advised as it forces out hands that I currently beat while committing me to a potentially large pot out of position. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings the Eight of Spades, creating a board of Ts-2h-9c-8s. I have a pair of 9s. The board is now quite coordinated, with straight draws present (like J-7 or 7-6). Since I am out of position against the button (Flash), leading out with a bet is risky because it bloats the pot with a marginal hand and allows the opponent to play perfectly against me. Checking allows me to control the pot size and act based on the opponent's reaction. If they check back, I get to see a river for free. If they bet, I can re-evaluate the price and the strength of their range.

On this turn, the 8s improves my hand to a pair of eights while maintaining my open-ended straight draw (6 or J). Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting they may have a marginal hand like a 9 or a Ten, or perhaps a draw. While I could bet for value or to semi-bluff, checking back here is a solid play. It allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free, ensuring I don't get check-raised off my draw. If I hit the straight on the river, I can still extract value, and if I miss, my pair of eights has some showdown value against their missed draws.

The board is Ts-2h-9c-8s-4c. I hold a pair of 9s with a King kicker, which has modest showdown value. The turn went check-check, suggesting that my opponent either has a marginal hand or missed a draw. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, as I don't have enough strength for a value bet. Checking allows me to control the pot size, reach showdown affordably, and potentially induce a bluff from a missed straight draw or overcards.

I have a pair of eights on a board with several overcards (10, 9). Flashlight has checked the river after checking the turn. While I might have the best hand against missed draws, I'm unlikely to get value from worse hands by betting. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and potentially win the pot with my pair without risking more chips against a marginal made hand like a 9 or a weak 10.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit in a three-handed game. While K-6 offsuit is not a premium hand, it is perfectly acceptable to open in position to steal the blinds, particularly as the current chip leader. I will open to 250 to apply pressure to the small blind and big blind.
I am in the Small Blind facing a 2.5bb raise from the Button. My hand, Ten-Deuce offsuit, is extremely weak and completely unplayable in this spot. Folding is the only reasonable option.

King-five offsuit is a marginal hand to defend from the Big Blind against a button raise. While the pot odds are roughly 2.6:1, the hand performs poorly out of position and is frequently dominated by the button's opening range. Given the stack depths and the tendency of opponents to be active, I prefer folding here to avoid being put in a difficult post-flop situation with a weak kicker.
Holding T6o, an unsuited and disconnected hand with weak high-card value. Being the first to act pre-flop, this is an easy fold from the Button.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 3c. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind, Flashlight, to act after me. While 93s has some equity against a random hand, it is a very weak starting hand that plays poorly out of position. Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands, and I would prefer not to engage in a post-flop battle out of position with such a weak holding. Since the stack sizes are deep (75 BBs), there is no pressure to play marginal or sub-par hands. Folding is the most disciplined choice here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Even from the button, there is no strategic advantage to playing this hand, as it has very poor equity and post-flop playability. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Button (Flash) has folded. I am in the Small Blind with King-Five offsuit. This is a standard spot to raise and take the initiative against the Big Blind (Pro), as playing a blind-versus-blind pot with the lead is statistically favorable. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts appropriate pressure on the Big Blind.

The Small Blind raised to 3x the big blind. Although I will have positional advantage post-flop, 74s is simply too weak to defend with by calling or 3-betting. It lacks sufficient high card strength, playability, and equity against a standard opening range. The most profitable play is to fold.

I am on the button with King-Three suited, which is a marginal hand but playable for a steal attempt in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is the standard aggressive play to contest the blinds or play the hand in position.

Ace-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a button raise, our main options are to 3-bet as a bluff or fold, as calling out of position with an easily dominated offsuit Ace is unprofitable and invites the Big Blind to squeeze. Without the equity realization of a suited Ace, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. Flashlight raised to 250 from the button, which is a standard steal attempt in 3-handed play. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 550, giving me pot odds of roughly 2.6:1 (requiring about 27% equity). While K5o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position, it is generally strong enough to defend against a wide button opening range in this format. I will call and see if I can hit top pair or a favorable board.

I have flopped middle pair (Kings) on an Ace-high board. Since Flashlight was the pre-flop aggressor from the button, this board texture significantly favors their range, which contains many strong Aces. I will check to the raiser to see how they proceed. My hand is strong enough to call a standard continuation bet, but leading out (donk betting) into the aggressor on this board is generally unwise given the range disadvantage.

On an Ace-high board (A-K-2), my pair of Kings is vulnerable. Checking behind is a prudent approach to control the pot size and keep my opponent's range wide, as betting might only be called by better hands like an Ace, while I can still realize my equity against bluffs or weaker pairs later.

On the turn, the board is Ks 2h Ah 8s. I have middle pair (Kings) with a 5 kicker. The pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, checked back on the flop, which is unusual for an Ace-high board if they held a strong Ace. This suggests they might have a marginal hand, a weak King, or air. I will check again to control the size of the pot and allow Flashlight to continue bluffing with inferior hands. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get value from worse hands, and I'd prefer to reach showdown cheaply given my weak kicker.

The board is Ace-high and my hand is only a pair of Kings with a weak kicker. Since my opponent checked to me on the turn, checking behind allows me to keep the pot controlled and realize my showdown equity without bloating the pot against potential hands that have me beat, like an Ace.

The river Queen of Hearts completes the board, making my hand a marginal third pair (Kings) behind Aces and Queens. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked back both the flop and the turn on a texture that usually warrants a continuation bet from a strong range. This passivity suggests they might have a weak showdown hand or a missed draw. However, since the Queen is now an overcard to my King and completes a potential flush, I have very little incentive to bet. A bet is unlikely to be called by worse hands or to fold out better ones. Checking is the best option to try and reach a cheap showdown.

I have a pair of Kings with a low kicker on an A-Q-K-8-2 board. My opponent has checked down the entire hand, signaling a likely weak or missed-draw range. Since any Ace or Queen has me beaten, and there are few hands worse than mine that would call a bet, checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown equity without bloating the pot against a potential trap or a range that just gives up.

I have a pocket pair of Deuces on the Button. This is a reasonable hand to open-raise to try and steal the blinds or see a flop to hit a set. Raising to 2.5x is standard. I'll raise to 250 to apply pressure while keeping the investment low.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 4s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While the hand is suited, 9-4 is extremely weak and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Calling here would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations against a competent opponent. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger opening or a better opportunity to defend.

Facing a standard button open from the Pro, calling with Ace-Four offsuit in the big blind is a standard defensive play. The pot odds are favorable (150 to win 400), and A4o is a strong enough hand to defend against a button raise, allowing me to see a flop with an Ace-high hand.

I hold the Ace of Diamonds, which gives me the nut flush draw on this monotone diamond board. Since I am out of position against the preflop aggressor (the button), the standard strategy is to check and evaluate their action. This allows me to keep the pot controlled while preparing to call a continuation bet or potentially raise if the odds/action warrant it.

I flopped bottom set of deuces. The board is monotone with all diamonds, meaning flush draws and made flushes are possible. Checking could give a free card to a single diamond, so I must bet to protect my equity and extract value from hands like top pair (Kx) or smaller pocket pairs with a diamond. A bet of around half the pot sizes up nicely to charge draws while keeping worse hands in.

I hold the Ace of Diamonds, which gives me the nut flush draw on this monotone diamond board. Since I am out of position against the preflop aggressor, calling the 300 bet is the standard play. This allows me to realize my equity and continue in the hand while keeping the pot controlled, as I have significant potential to hit the nut flush or possibly improve to a pair of Aces. Raising here is unnecessary and risky given the opponent could easily have a made hand or a better draw.

I hold the nut flush draw with the Ace of Diamonds on a board containing three diamonds. As I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and see the river card. If the opponent bets, I will have the odds to call or re-evaluate based on the size of the bet, and if they check behind, I get a free card to complete my flush.

I have bottom set on a monotone flop, and my opponent called my flop continuation bet. The Turn is the Ten of Hearts, and my opponent checks again. While I could bet for value and protection against one-diamond hands, my opponent's flop call indicates they either have a made flush, a strong draw, or at least a strong pair with a diamond. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, avoid getting check-raised out of my equity, and guarantee a free card to try and fill up to a full house on the river.

I have rivered the nut flush with the Ace of Diamonds on a board containing four diamonds. Since the turn was checked through, I am now first to act on the river. I have the absolute best possible hand, so I must value bet to extract chips. A bet of approximately half the pot (600 into 1150) is a reasonable sizing to encourage a call from hands like a made King, two-pair, or even a lower diamond flush that the opponent might have checked back on the turn.

The river brings a fourth diamond to the board. My opponent called my flop bet on a monotone diamond flop, strongly indicating they hold at least one diamond or already flopped a flush. When they lead out for 600 on the river, my set of deuces is completely crushed by any hand containing a diamond. Since the board didn't pair, I missed my full house outs, and with no diamonds in my hand, my set is merely a bluff catcher that loses to their entire value range. Folding is the only prudent action here.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce of spades. While 3-handed play requires a wider opening range, J2s is still at the bottom of the range and often results in difficult post-flop situations if called. Given that Flashlight and Pro have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet, I will choose the conservative route and fold this marginal hand to wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind position with a strong hand (King-Jack offsuit). Since the Button (Flash) has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). Raising is the optimal strategy here to exert pressure and either win the pot immediately or play a heads-up pot with a positional advantage. A raise to 300 is a standard 3x open size.

I hold 8c 5s, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, this hand has terrible equity and almost no playability post-flop. The clear and optimal decision here is to fold.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives, which is a strong starting hand. Raising allows me to take the betting initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or define my hand strength. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to open the action.

A7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Against a 3x raise from the Button, calling from the Small Blind is generally unprofitable because we will be out of position for the rest of the hand and the Big Blind could still squeeze. While a 3-bet bluff is possible against an active Button opener, we have better candidates in our range (like suited Aces or suited connectors). Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack of Clubs and Seven of Diamonds. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button and Pro folded. J7-offsuit is a weak hand to defend with, especially being out of position against the chip leader for the remainder of the hand. While the pot odds are roughly 31%, this hand realizes its equity poorly. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
With A5o on the Button in a 3-handed game, this is a clear opening hand. It blocks strong Aces and has decent playability. I will raise to 2.5x to pressure the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jh 5d, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250. Given that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand with a subpar range, there is no reason to defend this hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 7s. The button has raised to 250. Given the pot odds, calling is the standard and most profitable decision. My hand has good playability and implied odds against the button's opening range.

I am in the big blind with a flush draw on a paired board. Since I am out of position against the button, checking is the standard play to evaluate the action. A lead-out bet is unnecessary as the board texture is relatively dry and favors the button's range, and I want to keep the pot small while taking a free card if the opponent checks behind.

On a paired board like Q-Q-2, the pre-flop raiser maintains a strong range advantage. While my Ace-high has marginal showdown value, a small continuation bet applies pressure to the Big Blind's random floats, King-highs, and various drawing hands that have live equity against me. A bet of roughly 1/3 to 1/2 pot is an efficient price to fold out these better hands or hands with good equity, and the 5 of spades gives us a tiny bit of backdoor flush equity. If called, I can easily shut down and re-evaluate on the turn.

The opponent has bet 200 into a 750 pot, offering me excellent pot odds (roughly 3.75-to-1) to continue with my flush draw. Although the board is paired with Queens, making it possible for the opponent to have trips, my flush draw has sufficient equity to warrant a call. Raising would be ill-advised as it forces me to bloat the pot against a range that potentially crushes me. Calling is the most disciplined and mathematically sound action to realize my equity.

I am in the big blind with a flush draw on a paired board. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard play to evaluate the opponent's action. The board is paired (Queens), making it risky to lead out, and my flush draw is not the nuts, so I want to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity as cheaply as possible.

On the turn, the 8c doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does hit some of the Big Blind's floating range (like 89, 78). The opponent called our flop continuation bet, meaning their range consists of pocket pairs (33-JJ), flush draws, and potentially a slow-played Queen or an 8. Our Ace-high still has some showdown value against busted draws, but we lack equity against their calling range if we bet. By checking behind, we control the size of the pot, take a free card to try and hit our Ace, and retain the chance to win at showdown or bluff on the river if the situation warrants it.

I am in the big blind with 9-high on a paired board. I have no showdown value, and the board texture makes it difficult to represent a strong hand effectively. The opponent showed weakness by checking the turn, which makes it unlikely they will fold a hand with showdown value like Ace-high or King-high to a bluff. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize losses and avoid bloating the pot with a hand that loses to almost their entire continuing range.

On the river, the board runs out fairly cleanly with the 3d, and Flashlight checks to us once more. Our Ace-high retains decent showdown value against missed flush draws (like King or Jack-high spades) and random floats. If we bet, we are very unlikely to get called by worse hands, and it would be difficult to fold out marginal made hands like small pocket pairs (44-77), a 2, or an 8, especially since our turn check signaled weakness. Therefore, checking behind to realize our showdown value is the optimal play.

Holding Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a premium position. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage against the blinds.

I am in the small blind holding 8-5 suited. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against a button raise. Given that I am the chip leader, there is no need to contest speculative pots out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Q7s is a marginal hand, and against a 3x open from the Button, playing out of position with a gap like this can lead to difficult spots post-flop. While it has some suited connectivity, folding is the disciplined and optimal play here to preserve my stack.

76o is a weak offsuit connector that does not warrant an open-raise from the button in this 3-handed configuration. Folding allows me to preserve my chip lead and wait for a more advantageous hand to utilize my position.

With Queen-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) puts pressure on Flash to defend with marginal hands and allows me to take the initiative going into the flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 5c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 65-offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given that I will be acting first on every subsequent street and the hand has little high-card value or nut potential, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

92s is a very weak hand, even on the button. There is no need to try and steal the blinds with such low equity and playability when we can wait for better spots to utilize our position. Folding is the optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 6c, which is a very weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 36o performs poorly post-flop and I will be out of position. Given the Big Blind's large stack and active play, it is better to fold this trash hand and wait for a more profitable situation.

I am on the button with K6o. In a 3-handed game, while King-high hands are often worth opening to steal the blinds, K6o is a marginal hand that is easily dominated if called. Given I am currently the shortest stack of the three, I will choose to play conservatively and fold this hand, waiting for a stronger range to commit chips against the larger stacks of Flashlight and Pro.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket Kings (Kc, Ks), a premium hand. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I want to build a pot and assert control, so I will open-raise to 300, which is a standard sizing to isolate and value-bet.

Flashlight is frequently attempting to steal from the small blind when folded to. A5o is well ahead of a wide steal range. By calling, we keep their weaker hands in the pot and can utilize our positional advantage post-flop. Calling is a solid, low-variance option here.

I have an overpair with pocket Kings on a 6s Ts 5c board. While the board is not heavily coordinated, there are potential straight draws and flush draws that I want to charge for continuing. A continuation bet is standard here to extract value from pairs like 88, 99, or a ten and to protect my equity. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to maintain initiative and set up a strong line.

We flopped bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a moderately connected board that includes a flush draw. Facing a continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot, continuing is marginal. While we have position and might be ahead of some pure bluffs, we will face tough decisions against further aggression on many turn cards. Folding preserves our stack for higher-equity situations.

I am in the button position with Queen-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak hand with low playability. Given the strength of the hand, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack rather than opening with a marginal holding, especially since I have plenty of chips to wait for a better opportunity.
With A2o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a weak Ace but good steal equity. I will raise to 300 to pressure the Big Blind into folding, while still having high-card value if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Jack of hearts, a very strong broadway hand that plays excellently post-flop. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a blind-vs-blind battle, QJs is well ahead of a typical SB raising range. I will have position throughout the hand, which is a significant advantage. I choose to call the raise rather than 3-bet to keep Pro's range wide and utilize my position to outplay him on future streets.

With bottom pair, an overcard, and a gutshot straight draw on a wet board, checking is the safest play. It allows me to control the pot size out of position and evaluate Flash's action, keeping my options open for a call if the bet is reasonable.

Pro raised from the Small Blind and then checked a very low flop of 2s-5s-3c. This board is unlikely to have connected with a standard Small Blind raising range, which typically consists of high cards and pairs. By checking, Pro may be signaling a missed hand or a desire to pot control with a marginal holding. Since I have position and two overcards (Queen and Jack) with backdoor flush potential, I will place a probe bet of 300 (half the pot). This puts pressure on Pro's high-card hands like AK or AT and allows me to take down the pot immediately if they have missed. If called, I still have equity to improve on the turn.

Facing a half-pot bet on a wet 2s-5s-3c flop, I have bottom pair, top kicker (Ace), and a gutshot straight draw. With decent pot odds, folding is too tight, while raising could isolate me against stronger hands or draws that won't fold. Calling is the best option to evaluate the turn and keep his bluffs or weaker draws in the pot.

With bottom pair and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 2s 5s 3c Td, I am out of position. Checking is the standard play as my hand is not strong enough to value bet, and leading out as a bluff is risky against a player who bet the flop. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's action on the turn.

After my flop bet was called, the Ten of Diamonds on the turn doesn't significantly change the board texture or improve my hand. Pro's check-call on the flop suggests a marginal hand like a small pair or a draw. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get a free card to potentially hit a Queen or Jack on the river, which would likely give me the best hand. It also allows me to see how Pro acts on the river before deciding whether to bluff or fold.

With bottom pair (Deuces) on a 2-5-3-T-6 board where the obvious straight draw (4x) just completed, I have no value to gain by betting. Checking is the standard line to exercise pot control, as no worse hand will call a bet and I am only beating missed flush draws or complete air. I will check to see what my opponent does and likely fold to significant aggression.

I have Queen-high, which has almost no showdown value. However, the board is 2s-5s-3c-Td-6h, and the action has gone check-call on the flop followed by checks on the turn and river from my opponent. While I could attempt a bluff to push him off an Ace-high or a small pocket pair, my check on the turn significantly weakened my perceived range. An opponent who check-calls the flop and then checks all the way to the river is often in 'bluff-catching' mode with a marginal hand. Given this is an early stage of the tournament, I will check behind and concede the pot if I am beaten rather than risking a large portion of my stack on a low-frequency bluff.

K5 offsuit is a weak hand even when playing 3-handed. While I have the button and could attempt a steal, the hand lacks playability if called, and I want to avoid tricky post-flop situations with marginal holdings. Folding is the most solid play here.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the Button has folded, King-high is a relatively strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or see a flop with a range advantage.

Flash raised from the small blind. I am in the big blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. With 2:1 pot odds to call, this is a standard defensive play. The hand has decent enough playability to see a flop, especially given the wide ranges typically seen in blind vs blind battles.

As the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind battle, I have the range advantage on this dry board. While I missed the flop, a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) is likely to force a fold from many hands in Flashlight's defending range that also missed this board, such as middle suited connectors or lower high cards.

I am facing a bet of 300 into a pot of 600 on a board of Q-4-2. My hand, Jack-Eight offsuit, has completely missed this board and offers no pair, flush draw, or straight draw potential. With no equity and being out of position, continuing with a junk hand is mathematically incorrect. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 2c 6h, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. In a three-handed game, while ranges are wider, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open raise or a call against the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, Jack-Five offsuit. Since the button has folded, it is heads-up against the big blind. Investing additional chips with such a low-equity hand out of position is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Holding Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, even in a 3-handed game. Investing chips into the pot with this holding is not a profitable long-term strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack for stronger starting hands.
82s is a very weak hand, even when suited, and being out of position against the Big Blind makes it difficult to play post-flop. I'll just fold and save the chips.

King-Ten suited is a strong starting hand, especially from the Button. Raising here is the standard play to steal the blinds or build a pot with a hand that plays very well post-flop. A raise to 3 big blinds (300) will thin the field and give us the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with 6-5 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. While 6-5 is a connector, playing it offsuit and out of position against a Button raise is generally a losing play. Additionally, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is still to act and could 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with 2h 5h. The button (Pro) raised to 300, and the small blind folded. I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds to call (200 to call for a 600 total pot). While 25 suited is a weak hand, it is suited and the price is right to defend my blind against a standard button open. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop.

I hold bottom pair (deuces) on a coordinated board (Js-Qd-2d). Given that the opponent (Pro) raised pre-flop, they likely hold high cards that may have connected with this board. My pair is very vulnerable, and betting into the pre-flop aggressor is not advisable. Checking is the standard approach to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

With Kc Tc on a Js Qd 2d flop, I have an open-ended straight draw. This is a very strong drawing hand that serves as an excellent semi-bluffing candidate. Since the Big Blind checked to me, a continuation bet puts pressure on their marginal holdings, like weak Jacks or underpairs, while also building the pot in case I hit my straight on the turn or river. Betting around half the pot is a standard c-bet sizing that gives a good risk-to-reward ratio for this semi-bluff.

I hold bottom pair on a coordinated board containing a Jack and a Queen, which are squarely in the opening range of the opponent. The opponent's continuation bet suggests they have connected with the board, or are C-betting with overcards, both of which likely have me beat or have significant equity against my weak pair. Given the lack of strong draws and the vulnerability of my hand, calling a bet here is unprofitable. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, J6o lacks the post-flop equity to justify an open-raise or a call, especially since both Flashlight and Pro have shown willingness to defend and play aggressively. As the shortest stack, I should be more selective with the hands I play to avoid bleeding chips.

I am in the small blind with a weak, suited hand (Qc 2c). Since the button folded, I have a heads-up situation against the big blind. Given the pot odds, completing the bet (calling the remaining 50) is a standard play to see a flop cheaply, as I am the chip leader and can afford to play a marginal hand in this position.
QJo is a strong hand to isolate a Small Blind limp. By raising to 300, I take the initiative, punish the passive play, and set myself up to leverage my positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the small blind with a weak, suited holding (Qc 2c). After completing the small blind, the big blind (Pro) raised to 300. Calling 200 more to see a flop out of position with such a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent is suboptimal. Folding is the most disciplined approach to maintain stack health.

Holding a weak hand like 3h 4c, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. I am on the button, but even with position, the hand has poor equity against any calling or raising range. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
63o is an extremely weak starting hand. Even in a blind vs blind scenario, it's not profitable to complete or raise with this hand. The best decision is simply to fold and save the remaining chips.

A8s is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. As the first to act on the Button, I want to open with a raise to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind accomplishes this.
