I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. Based on previous hands, Pro is quite active. I want to 3-bet here for value and to take initiative, rather than playing a bloated pot out of position. A 3-bet to 850 (similar to the sizing used in Hand #1) puts pressure on the Button's wide opening range and helps protect my hand against the Big Blind.

My hand (Jack-Eight offsuit) is very weak and lacks potential to play well in a bloated pot against two aggressive opponents. Facing an open from the button and a significant 3-bet from the small blind, my hand is dominated by the ranges likely held by both players. Continuing with this hand would be a negative expected value play as I would be out of position and likely to have a very difficult time navigating post-flop play. The most disciplined and profitable action is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, A8s is strong enough to defend in position in a 3-handed game. The hand has excellent playability post-flop with nut flush potential and top pair outs. Calling allows me to realize equity and utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot with a 4-bet.

I am the pre-flop 3-bettor and hold Ace-Jack offsuit. The flop of 6c-2h-5c is low and disconnected, which generally favors a 3-bettor's range of overpairs (88-AA) that the caller (Pro) likely doesn't have after just calling the 3-bet. While I missed the board, I have two overcards and want to maintain the initiative I established pre-flop. A continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot (720-750) is effective here to put pressure on Pro's mid-range hands and suited connectors, while also protecting my hand's equity against his overcards. I'll bet 750 to keep the pressure on.

I have the nut flush draw and an overcard to the board. Facing a standard continuation bet from the Small Blind, I have excellent pot odds and plenty of equity to continue. Calling in position keeps my opponent's bluffs in the hand, allows me to pot control, and ensures I realize my equity smoothly without over-committing against a strong made hand that might re-raise.

After my continuation bet on the flop was called, the turn is a Ten of Spades, which is relatively neutral. While I still have overcards, betting again (double barreling) into a range that called a 3-bet and a flop bet is risky with only Ace-high. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Pro reacts. This allows me to potentially see a river for free or evaluate a bet from Pro based on his sizing, while also protecting my checking range.

My opponent checking the turn gives me the perfect opportunity to take a free card with my nut flush draw. Checking behind guarantees that I fully realize my equity without risking a check-raise that could force me to fold my strong draw. It keeps the pot manageable, and if I hit my flush on the river, I can extract value then.

The board runout (6c-2h-5c-Ts-7s) didn't improve my Ace-Jack. I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, both of which Pro called, suggesting he has some piece of the board or a pocket pair. Pro checking back on the turn indicates he likely has some showdown value but isn't holding a monster. While Ace-high has limited showdown value, betting into this board now is unlikely to fold out better hands like middle pairs (88, 99) or any pair of 6s or 5s he might have called with. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, or fold if he makes a significant bet, as my hand is now just a bluff-catcher.

My opponent has checked both the turn and the river, signaling weakness and a likely capped range, such as missed broadway cards (AK, AQ, AJ) or a small-to-medium pocket pair. While I hold Ace-high, my 8 kicker is dominated by the stronger Aces in their pre-flop 3-betting range, meaning I will likely lose at showdown if we check it down. By turning my hand into a bluff with a solid river bet, I can apply pressure and force folds from better Ace-highs and potentially even marginal pairs that are afraid of the highly connected board, allowing me to take down a substantial pot.

I am currently holding Ace-high (AdJs). Pre-flop, I 3-bet and then continuation bet a low, dry flop, which Pro called. After both of us checked the turn, the river brought a 7s. Pro is now betting 1800 into a 3300 pot. While Pro has shown some aggression in previous hands, calling a significant river bet with only Ace-high is risky here. Pro's range for calling a 3-bet and a flop bet likely includes medium pocket pairs (77-JJ), suited connectors that have now paired (6x, 5x, 7x), or potentially a Ten that improved on the turn. While I am getting decent pot odds (about 3.8:1), it is unlikely my Ace-high is ahead of a range that called two streets of aggression, and I have no reason to believe Pro is over-bluffing this specific board texture after the turn went check-check. I will preserve my stack and fold.

I am on the button with 4h 3s, which is a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, 43o is at the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop. With a stack of over 50 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Flash folded on the button, leaving me in the small blind with Queen-Six offsuit. Since I have already posted 50 chips as the small blind, I only need to call 50 more to see a flop against the big blind. Completing the blind is a standard, low-risk strategic decision here given the marginal strength of the hand.

Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand. The Small Blind limped in, giving me the option to check and see a free flop. There is no strategic value in raising as a bluff with this hand, so checking to realize equity on the flop is the standard and most profitable decision.

The flop of 2d-Jc-9s is rainbow and completely missed my Queen-Six offsuit. With no pair, no significant draw, and no backdoor equity, I have no reason to bet. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

With Ten-high and no direct draw on a Jack-Nine-Deuce board, I have very little equity. Since Flashlight checked to me, there is no reason to inflate the pot or attempt a bluff with such a weak hand. Taking a free card is the best option to see if I can improve on the turn.

The board has paired on the turn with the nine of diamonds (2d-Jc-9s-9d). My hand (Qc-6h) has absolutely no showdown value and essentially zero equity against anything better than a complete airball. The big blind (Pro) has checked twice, which suggests they also do not have a strong hand (no Nine, likely no Jack). Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that could easily have a piece of the board or a small pair that won't fold. Checking is the most prudent play to keep the pot small and reach a cheap showdown.

With ten-high and no meaningful draw on a paired board, I have no showdown value and minimal equity. Flashlight has checked twice, but a bluff here with such a weak hand is unnecessary and risky. Checking to see a free river card is the optimal play to avoid inflating the pot with a worthless hand.

The board has run out with a pair of nines and a King. My Queen-high has no showdown value, but the board is extremely uncoordinated, and the action has been checked down entirely. Pro checked back their option in the big blind pre-flop and checked down every street. There is no reason to bluff here as I am likely against a hand that will not fold to a small bet (like a low pair or weak Jack) or a hand that already has me beaten. Checking is the only logical play to see a cheap showdown.

I have Ten-high on a board with a King, Jack, paired Nines, and three diamonds. I have absolutely no showdown value, and my only way to win this pot would be to bluff. However, since this was a limped pot that checked all the way down, Flashlight's range is wide and could easily contain a sticky Ace-high or a weak pair that might look to pick off a bluff. Given the small size of the pot, it's not worth risking chips on a low-probability bluff. Checking behind and surrendering the pot is the safest and most standard decision.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a strong hand for this position and warrants an opening raise to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds. 300 is a standard opening size based on previous hand history.

With JTo in the Small Blind facing a 3x open from the Button, folding is the standard and safest play. Playing a marginal offsuit broadway hand out of position against the pre-flop raiser, with the Big Blind still to act, is a losing proposition in the long run.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5d 7d, a suited gapped connector. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300, and Pro (Small Blind) folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. The pot odds are 2.25:1, meaning I need approximately 31% equity to justify a call. 5d 7d is a decent hand to defend in a heads-up pot against a Button open, as the Button's range is likely wide and the suited nature of my hand provides good playability on many flop textures. With 50 BBs remaining, I have enough depth to play post-flop.

I have bottom pair (Fives) on an Ace-high board that heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Flashlight on the Button). Leading into the aggressor here makes little sense as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. I will check to see how the Button responds; if they check back, I have some showdown value, and if they bet, I will evaluate the sizing to see if defending bottom pair is profitable.

I have top pair (Ace) with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry board. Flash checked to me, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet for value and protection. A sizing of 350 into the 650 pot is an effective continuation bet to pressure potential Queens, middle pairs, or drawing hands while building a pot with the best hand.

I have bottom pair on an Ace-high board. While this board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, a bet of 350 into 650 is a standard continuation bet that could be made with many air hands or draws in a Button's range. I am getting roughly 3.8:1 odds to call. Folding bottom pair immediately would be over-folding against a wide Button opening range. I will call this bet and re-evaluate on the turn, looking to fold to further aggression unless my hand improves.

The turn pairs the Queen, which is a card that hits the pre-flop aggressor's (Button) range significantly well. My bottom pair of fives now has very little value and is behind many hands in the opponent's range, including any Ace, any Queen, and any pocket pair higher than fives. I will check to the aggressor. If Flashlight bets again, I will likely fold, as it is difficult to justify calling another bet with such a weak hand on this board texture.

I have two pair (Aces and Queens) with an Ace-Jack kicker. The turn paired the Queen, which is a very strong board for me, as it makes it unlikely for the opponent to have a better hand unless they hold a Queen for trips. Given that Flash called my flop bet, he likely has an Ace with a weaker kicker, a pocket pair, or a draw. Betting for value is the standard play here to extract chips from those holdings.

On a board of Ah-Qd-5s-Qh, my bottom pair of fives is extremely vulnerable. The Queen pairing the turn is much better for the pre-flop aggressor's range (Flashlight) than it is for mine. Flashlight's second barrel of 650 into a pot of 1350 is a significant bet. I am currently losing to any Ace, any Queen, and any pocket pair higher than fives. Given the board texture and the betting action, it is highly likely I am beat. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds, my hand has almost no improvement potential and is a weak bluff-catcher at best. I will fold to preserve my remaining 4500 chips.
K4s is a reasonable suited hand to open-raise from the Button in a 3-handed game to steal the blinds. Raising to 2.5x the big blind applies pressure while keeping the risk relatively low.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Jack offsuit, a strong hand in a three-handed game. The button (Pro) raised to 250, which is a standard opening size. With a stack of 4450 chips (44.5 BB), calling to see a flop is a solid play that keeps the pot manageable while still allowing me to realize my hand's equity. 3-betting is also an option, but given my position and stack depth relative to the chip leaders, calling allows me to evaluate the board before committing more chips.

With 5:1 pot odds (150 to win 600) and having a large stack, defending my Big Blind with a suited connector is a standard, profitable play despite the marginal strength of Queen-Three suited. I have position on the post-flop betting rounds, allowing me to potentially exploit the opponents if I hit a pair or a flush draw.

On a flop of 7h-7s-5s, I have King-high (Kd-Js). This board is paired and contains a flush draw. As the first to act out of position against two opponents, checking is the most prudent play. I have two overcards but no direct piece of this board, and I want to see how the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and the Big Blind (Flashlight) react before committing more chips to the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on a paired board (7-7-5). I have no pair and no flush draw. There is no reason to lead out with a bet into two opponents, one of whom was the pre-flop raiser. Checking is the standard play to re-evaluate on the turn.

With King-high and no draws on a paired, wet board (two spades) in a multi-way pot, a continuation bet is unlikely to succeed against two opponents who called a pre-flop raise. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and avoids bloat the pot when I have very little equity.

The turn brings the Ace of Spades, which is a significant card as it completes potential flushes and is a high overcard. Given that the flop checked around, it is possible that an opponent is slow-playing a monster or has improved. With King-high and no spades in my hand, I have no reason to bet into two opponents. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the action develops.

I am sitting with Queen-high on a board with three spades and a paired seven. I have no pair and no spade in my hand, meaning I have no showdown value and no flush draw. There is no strategic incentive to bet here as I will likely be called by any Ace or any pair, and I cannot realistically represent a flush or a full house. Checking is the only logical move to see the river for free.

The turn brings an Ace of Spades, completing the flush draw and putting a scary overcard on the board. Holding only King-high with no draws, my hand has virtually no equity. While both opponents checking again could indicate weakness, bluffing into two players on this wet, paired board is unnecessarily risky. Checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to see a free river card.

The board has double-paired with Aces and Sevens (7h-7s-5s-As-Ac). My best five-card hand is now Aces and Sevens with a King kicker (AA77K). Given that all players have checked on every street (Flop, Turn, and now the action is on me at the River), it is highly unlikely that anyone is holding an Ace or a Seven. My King kicker is very strong in this 'checked-down' scenario, as I beat any hand that is playing the board (AA775) or any hand with a lower kicker (like QJ or JT). I will check to see a showdown, as betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and I have sufficient showdown value to win the pot.

The board is A-A-7-7-5. With my hole cards (Q-3), my best 5-card hand is A-A-7-7-Q. My Queen kicker is actually quite strong here because any hand holding a card lower than a Queen (that does not contain an Ace or King) will lose to me. Since betting would likely only be called by a hand containing an Ace or King (which beats me) and would cause hands that I beat to fold, checking is the optimal strategy to reach showdown and capitalize on my hand's relative value.

The board has double paired with Aces and Sevens. Since neither opponent has shown aggression on any street, it is very unlikely anyone holds an Ace or a Seven. My King plays as an excellent kicker to the board's two pair (A-A-7-7-K). If I bet, better hands (like pocket 8s through Queens) might call, and worse hands (like Queen-high or counterfeit low pocket pairs) will fold. Since I have significant showdown value but no value to extract from worse hands, checking behind is the optimal play to realize my equity.

I am on the button with Qh Ts, which is a solid hand in a three-handed game. My stack is the shortest at 46 big blinds, so I need to play proactively to maintain my position. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take control of the hand and use my positional advantage against Flashlight and Pro.

I am in the small blind with a weak offsuit hand (4sTh) facing a button raise. Playing out of position with this hand is negative expected value and offers poor playability post-flop. The most disciplined action is to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.
K2o is a very weak hand to defend against a 3x button open. It plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Opening the pot from the button is standard strategy to take initiative and steal the blinds. A raise to 300 is a solid sizing to accomplish this goal while building a pot for when I have the best hand.

I am in the small blind with 72s, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the button, there is no justification to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a chip leader who has been aggressive. With a stack of 4675 (approx. 47 BB), I prefer to wait for a better hand or a more advantageous situation to commit chips rather than defending a marginal hand like this.

I am on the button with 6h 9c, an unsuited and disconnected weak hand. In a 3-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify opening or limping, so the optimal choice is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. J5o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Given my stack size of approximately 46 big blinds and the relatively passive to aggressive nature of Flashlight in the BB, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger opening or a better position to commit chips.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey suited. While being suited provides some playability, the hand is fundamentally weak with a very low kicker. Calling is the most balanced approach to see the river and evaluate the final board texture.

The river pairs the board with the Ten, introducing the possibility of full houses for my opponent, such as AT, TT, 55, or 66. However, my King-Queen of clubs forms the nut flush (as the Ace of clubs is on the board), which remains extremely strong. I need to go for value against worse hands in his range, like smaller flushes, trips (JT, T9), and potentially sticky Aces. Checking might allow him to comfortably check behind with these marginal hands. A bet of exactly half the pot targets these bluff-catchers for a call without scaring them away, maximizing value while avoiding over-committing with a shove in case he actually rivered a boat.

The board features paired Tens, which significantly devalues my top pair. Pro has demonstrated consistent aggression throughout all three betting streets, which strongly indicates a made hand, likely trips with a Ten or a better Ace. With such a weak 8-kicker, my hand is almost certainly behind, and calling a significant river bet as a bluff-catcher against such a strong range is not mathematically sound. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent action.
Holding a strong pocket pair like JJ on the Button, I want to raise for value to build the pot and thin the field. A standard raise of 2.5x the big blind is appropriate.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak hand to play from the small blind against a button raise, especially with the chip leader opening. It does not play well post-flop out of position, and I should preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight suited, a reasonable hand to defend against a button open raise. The cost to call is 150 into a pot of 400, offering good pot odds. Folding would be too passive, and while 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to control the pot size and play post-flop with a decent holding.

I have top pair (King) and a flush draw on a relatively dry but coordinated board. Checking out of position might allow my opponent (who is aggressive) to check back, losing value. I should lead out with a bet to define my hand strength, build the pot, and protect against free cards for opponents with draws. A bet of 300 into a 550 pot is a standard sizing to accomplish these goals.

Flashlight's donk bet on a K-high board with a flush draw can represent a wide range of hands, including weaker pairs, flush draws, or a King. With pocket Jacks, my hand is too strong to fold, but raising could isolate me against hands that beat me (like Kx or sets). Calling controls the pot size, keeps his bluffs and draws in the hand, and allows me to evaluate on the turn in position.

I have top pair with a King and an open-ended/flush draw potential (though only the flush draw is strong here). The turn pairing the trey is relatively safe, as it makes trips less likely for the opponent. I want to continue building the pot for value while charging for potential draws. Betting 600 into the 1150 pot maintains pressure and sets up a reasonable river scenario.

The turn pairs the 3, which doesn't change much unless Flashlight specifically had exactly a 3, which is unlikely given his pre-flop call and flop donk bet. His double barrel could still represent a King, but on a draw-heavy board (two hearts, two diamonds, straight draws), he could easily be semi-bluffing with a flush draw or a worse pair like 88-TT or 7x. Since I have pocket Jacks, my hand is too strong to fold to a half-pot bet. I will call to keep his bluffs in and evaluate on the river.

I have reached the river with a pair of Kings (Kh-Kd). The board is paired (3s), and the flush draw has missed. My opponent has called bets on both the flop and turn, which indicates they have at least a pair or are drawing. A King with an Eight kicker is a medium-strength hand that is likely best, but if I bet, I am likely only called by better hands (like a King with a better kicker or trips) and I fold out worse hands that might bluff if checked to. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from the opponent's missed flush draws. Checking is the most standard and profitable play here.

Flashlight's decision to check the river after double-barreling suggests he has either given up on a missed draw (like a heart or diamond flush draw) or is trying to pot-control with a marginal made hand (like 7x, 88-TT, or a weak King). With pocket Jacks, my hand has strong showdown value, but betting is unlikely to get called by many worse hands and risks running into a trap or a stronger holding like Kx. Checking behind allows me to safely realize my equity and win the pot against his bluffs or weaker pairs.
Pocket nines is a strong premium hand in a 3-handed game, especially from the button. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and exert positional pressure on the blinds.

KQs is a high-equity hand in a three-handed game. Being in the Small Blind against a button open, a 3-bet is the standard professional play to take control of the hand, define the opponent's range, and discourage the Big Blind from coming along. Raising to 900 increases the stack-to-pot ratio in my favor and capitalizes on my positional disadvantage by establishing initiative.

Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, A6o is far too weak to continue from the Big Blind. I am out of position against both players and dominated by many hands in their 3-betting ranges. The standard play is to fold.

I am holding pocket nines on the button. This is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Flashlight's three-bet from the small blind indicates strength, but folding would be overly tight given my position and the hand strength. A four-bet might over-commit my stack or force out hands that I currently beat. Calling allows me to play the pot in position and see a flop, where I can better evaluate Flashlight's range.

I have top pair with a good kicker on a relatively dry board. As the 3-bettor, I have the initiative. A continuation bet is standard here to extract value from lower pairs or gutshot draws like Q10 or 109, and to force folds from hands that might have equity against me. I will size the bet to about one-third of the pot to keep the action manageable and maintain control.

On a board of Kh-Js-4d, my pocket nines have been significantly devalued following a 3-bet pre-flop. The board contains two overcards that hit a typical small blind 3-betting range (AK, KQ, KJ, AJ, KK, JJ). Flashlight's continuation bet of 600 into 1900 is small, but given the board texture and the fact that I am likely drawing to only two outs if behind, continuing here is unprofitable. I will fold and preserve my remaining 4,925 chips for a better spot.

I am on the button in a three-handed game, which is the most advantageous position. With King-high (Kh3c), although not a premium hand, it is strong enough to open from the button to steal the blinds or take the initiative. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a strategic play to pressure the blinds, particularly the short-stacked Flash, and potentially win the pot pre-flop.
I have T7o in the small blind facing a button open. This hand is way too weak to call or 3-bet, so folding is the straightforward and optimal choice.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Jd Jc) in the Big Blind. This is a premium hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Flashlight opened to 250 from the Button. Since I have a stack of approximately 48 Big Blinds, I want to play this hand aggressively to extract value and protect against overcards. A 3-bet is the standard play here. I will raise to 900 to build the pot and define Flashlight's range.

Facing a 3-bet from the big blind after my initial button raise, K3 offsuit is not a playable hand. The strength of a 3-betting range, even from an aggressive opponent, heavily dominates my hand. Continuing here would be mathematically unprofitable and unnecessary given the stack sizes, so folding is the disciplined choice.

With A4o on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a clear opening hand. The Ace serves as a good blocker, and I will have positional advantage post-flop against the blinds. I'll raise to a standard 2.5x sizing (250) to attempt to steal the blinds or take control of the hand.

I have Ac Jc in the small blind, which is a premium hand. The button (Pro) opened for 250. I should 3-bet here to build value and take initiative. A raise to 950 is a standard size to discourage the big blind from coming along cheaply and to put pressure on the button's opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen of Hearts and Five of Spades. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flash) 3-bet to 950. My hand is extremely weak (non-suited, unconnected, low side card) and I am out of position against two players. Calling or raising here would be a significant mistake as my hand will rarely win against such a strong betting range. Folding is the only disciplined and strategically sound decision.

I opened A4o on the button, which is a standard steal attempt. Flash, in the Small Blind, 3-bet to 9.5 BB. A4o is simply too weak to defend against a 3-bet, as it will often be dominated by better Aces or pocket pairs in Flash's range, and it lacks the playability to call profitably. Furthermore, Flash started the hand with around 40 BB, meaning we don't have the implied odds to set-mine or outplay him post-flop. The clear choice is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8s 6h. While opening a wide range on the button is standard in 3-handed play, 86-offsuit is at the bottom of that range. Both Flashlight and Pro have shown significant aggression and a tendency to 3-bet or defend their blinds recently. Given that I am the shortest stack at the table (though still at 55bb), I prefer to fold these marginal hands and wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot out of position or folding to a 3-bet.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (Jack-high, offsuit). Folding is the standard play here to avoid playing a poor hand out of position against the big blind, especially since I have already committed the small blind and don't need to risk further chips.

I am on the button with Js 9s, a strong suited connector with good post-flop potential. In position, this is an excellent hand to open, applying pressure to the blinds and potentially winning the pot pre-flop or seeing a favorable flop. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative.
Holding T3o in the Small Blind against a Button raise is a clear fold. The hand has almost no playability or equity, and calling out of position would be a massive mistake.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is the statistically weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise. Although the pot odds for a call are somewhat tempting, playing the worst hand in the game out of position against a stack that covers me is not a profitable strategy. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
With pocket nines on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a very strong starting hand. I will make a standard opening raise of 2.5x the big blind to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven of hearts, a strong suited Ace in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise to 250. While calling is an option, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately, especially since I've had success with this move in recent hands (Hands #76 and #77). A7s has good blockers and playability. I will raise to 950 to put pressure on the Button and define their range.

I am in the big blind with Kd 8c, facing a button open to 250 and a 3-bet from the small blind to 950. My hand is not strong enough to contest a 3-bet pot out of position, especially against a player who has shown aggression. Calling would commit a significant portion of my stack with a dominated hand, and 4-betting as a bluff is ill-advised against two opponents. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Flash has shown a tendency to 3-bet aggressively from the blinds in recent hands. With pocket nines, I have a strong hand that plays well against a wide 3-betting range. However, a 4-bet might isolate me against higher pairs (TT+) or strong broadways, and with Flash's stack being relatively short, it could lead to an all-in pre-flop coin flip. By calling in position, I keep their bluffs in the pot, control the variance, and can evaluate the flop. I will look to continue if the board runs out cleanly without too many overcards.

I have top pair with an Ace, but the board texture (Q-A-K) is extremely coordinated and hits a 3-bet calling range very hard. Hands like AK, AQ, and JT (a straight) are well within the opponent's range. With my weak kicker (7) and relatively short stack compared to the pot, I want to exercise pot control and see how the opponent reacts before committing more chips. Checking allows me to evaluate the situation while protecting my checking range.

The flop of Qd Ac Kh is a nightmare for pocket nines. It hits almost all of my opponent's 3-betting range (AK, AQ, KQ, AA, KK, QQ, AJ, AT, etc.) heavily. Even though Flash checked, betting here would only fold out worse hands (if any exist) and get called or raised by better ones. Taking a free card is the safest and most logical play.

The board remains very coordinated (Q-A-K-8), and although I have top pair, my kicker (7) is very weak. Pre-flop, the Button called my 3-bet, suggesting a range that contains many broadway cards, some of which (like AJ, AT, KQ) are currently beating me. The check on the flop by both of us suggests either weakness or a desire for pot control. By checking the turn, I continue to exercise pot control and keep the pot manageable given my stack size (SPR around 2.2). I want to see if the opponent bets, which might help define their hand, or if they check back again, potentially taking us to a relatively cheap showdown.

The turn card is an 8s, keeping the board extremely dangerous for my pocket nines (Qd Ac Kh 8s). While Flash has checked twice, a bet from me serves no purpose: better hands like Ax, Kx, Qx, or a straight will never fold, and the only hands that might fold are those I'm already beating, like missed bluffs or smaller pairs (which are rare in a 3-bet range on this board). Checking behind to control the pot size and hopefully get to a cheap showdown is the most sensible play.

The river Jack is a very dangerous card as any Ten now makes a straight (TJ, TQ, TK, TA are all within Pro's pre-flop calling range). Furthermore, this board is heavily coordinated with broadway cards, and nearly all the hands Pro would call a 3-bet with pre-flop (AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ, QJ) now have me beaten with two pair, a straight, or a better one pair. My top pair with a 7 kicker is now purely a bluff-catcher at best. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, but I am likely behind.

The river brings a Jack, putting four to a straight on the board (A-K-Q-J). Flash has checked all three streets after 3-betting pre-flop, which typically indicates marginal showdown value like a weak Ace, a King, or a medium pocket pair that got scared by the board. My pocket nines have absolutely no showdown value here, as any pair, higher kicker, or a Ten beats me. However, bluffing into a 4-to-a-straight board against a 3-betting range is highly unprofitable, as Flash is likely to call with their one-pair hands like AK or AQ. Checking behind and accepting the loss is the most risk-free and sensible decision.

I have a premium starting hand (Ace-Jack suited) on the button. In a three-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand with the benefit of being in position for the remainder of the hand. This sizing is consistent with my previous opening raises.

Flash has opened from the button, and I am in the small blind with Ten of Clubs and Nine of Clubs. This hand is well-suited for calling in this position to see a flop, as it plays well in multi-way pots. While I will be out of position, the stack-to-pot ratio is reasonable, allowing me to potentially realize my equity or outmaneuver the opener on a favorable board.

Holding pocket Kings in the Big Blind after a Button open and a Small Blind call is a prime spot for a value squeeze. Squeezing large here penalizes the SB's capped range and puts maximum pressure on the Button, while building a big pot with a premium hand. I'll 3-bet to 1300, which is over 4x the original raise to account for being out of position.

Ace-Jack suited is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I originally opened from the button, and while Pro has put in a significant 3-bet from the big blind, I have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. Calling the raise is the most prudent play; it keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop. 4-betting might force me to commit too much of my stack against a potentially superior hand like Ace-King or a high pocket pair, whereas calling allows me to see a flop and make a more informed decision.

I am facing a squeeze raise from the Big Blind after the Button opened and I called. With Ten of Clubs and Nine of Clubs in the Small Blind, I am getting decent pot odds (roughly 2.9:1) to make the call of 1000 chips. While playing out of position in a three-bet pot is challenging, T9s has strong connectivity and multi-way potential. Given my stack size, calling is the most balanced approach to realize my equity and see a flop.

I am in the small blind, first to act on the flop, with T9s on a board of Qh-4h-7c. My hand has no pairs and no significant draws. In a three-way pot, betting into two opponents with only ten-high is mathematically weak. Checking is the standard, disciplined approach here to minimize potential losses and evaluate the actions of my opponents. If there is heavy betting action following my check, I will be prepared to fold.

With an overpair (Kings) on a Qh 4h 7c board, I have a very strong hand but need to charge flush draws and single pair hands like AQ or KQ. The pot is already large (3900) and the Button's remaining stack is 5225, making the SPR quite low. A bet of around 1500 sets up an easy turn shove against the Button, while extracting value from worse queens, medium pocket pairs, and heart draws.

On the flop of Qh 4h 7c, I have the nut flush draw with Ace-Jack of hearts. The pre-flop action was heavy, with a 3-bet from the big blind (Pro) that both myself and the small blind (Flashlight) called. Pro has now led out for 1500 into a 3900 pot. This is a strong continuation bet into two players, suggesting a range like AQ, KK, AA, or perhaps a set. I have approximately 36% equity just from the flush draw, and my Ace or Jack might occasionally be good as well. With 5225 chips remaining, calling the 1500 is the most logical choice. It allows me to see the turn in position while keeping the pot-to-stack ratio manageable. Shoving is an option to maximize fold equity against hands like AK or middle pairs, but Pro's 3-bet range is likely strong enough to call a shove. By calling, I also see what Flashlight does; if I hit my flush on the turn, I am in a great position to extract maximum value from both opponents.

I am facing a bet from the Big Blind and a call from the Button on a board of Qh-4h-7c. My hand, Ten of Clubs and Nine of Clubs, has essentially no equity—I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush draw. With two opponents who have shown continued aggression and interest in the pot, my ten-high hand is dominated. The pot odds do not justify a call given the lack of outs. Folding is the only disciplined and strategically correct decision to preserve my remaining stack for better opportunities.

With the pot at 6900 and Flash having exactly 3725 left behind, the SPR is around 0.5. My pocket Kings remain an extremely strong overpair on this Qh 4h 7c Td board. The Ten on the turn does not complete the main flush draw and mostly improves hands like QT or TT, but Flash's range is heavily weighted towards hands like AQ, KQ, or various heart flush draws that he peeled with on the flop. Checking risks a check-back from draws, missing value and giving a free river card. Betting his remaining stack puts him all-in, extracting maximum value from top pairs and charging his draws.

On the turn, the board is Qh 4h 7c Td. I have the nut flush draw with Ah Jh. Pro has shoved for my remaining 3725 chips into a pot of 6900, making the total pot 10625 if I call. To call, I need 3725 / (10625 + 3725) ≈ 26% equity. With only the 9 hearts as certain outs (19.5% equity), I am mathematically behind. While my Ace or Jack could potentially be outs, Pro's 3-bet pre-flop and strong lead on both the flop and turn strongly suggest a range of AQ, AA, KK, or even a set like 77 or TT, most of which have my Ace/Jack outs crushed. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 3725 chips (37.25 big blinds), whereas calling is a negative expected value play that risks my entire stack on a sub-26% draw.

Pocket Jacks are a premium pocket pair and a very strong hand in this position. As the first player to act on the button, opening with a raise is the standard and correct strategy to build the pot and assert initiative. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is an appropriate sizing to either pick up the blinds or extract value from opponents.

T7o is a weak offsuit hand, and facing a raise from the button while in the small blind, the standard and most profitable play is to fold. Playing this hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser would lead to a negative expected value.

I am holding 92 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although the pot odds are roughly 3-to-1 to call the button raise, this hand has extremely poor playability and equity realization, especially out of position. Given my current stack size of 36 BBs, it's more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit chips.

K8o is a marginal hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. While it could be used as a steal, both opponents have playable stacks and there are better spots to get involved. I prefer folding here to maintain a tight-aggressive image and avoid marginal situations post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 4-6 offsuit after the Button has folded. While it only costs 50 more to call, 4-6o is a very weak hand and I will be playing out of position against Flashlight, who has a significantly larger stack. It is better to fold and preserve my 35 BB stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. This is a weak starting hand, even for a button opening range. Given the aggressive play from both Flashlight and Pro in recent hands, particularly their tendency to 3-bet or put on pressure post-flop, playing this hand out of position or in a bloated pot is likely to lead to a loss. I will wait for a better spot to commit my remaining 35 big blinds.

Pocket Jacks are a very strong hand heads-up against the Big Blind. Since the button has folded, I want to take the initiative and build the pot. Raising to 300 is a standard, aggressive move to capitalize on my premium pair.

With KQo in the big blind facing a small blind raise, I have a very strong hand that dominates many of the small blind's opening hands (like weaker Kx and Qx). 3-betting for value and to take the initiative is a strong play blind-versus-blind. I'll raise to 900 to define his range and potentially take the pot down now, or play a larger pot in position.

Pocket Jacks are a strong premium hand, and facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my open from the Small Blind, I should 4-bet to regain the initiative and define the opponent's range. Flat-calling out of position against a 3-bet is dangerous as it makes post-flop play difficult if an overcard appears. A 4-bet to 2,200 is a standard sizing to pressure the opponent and value-bet my premium pair.

Facing a 4-bet from the small blind, my KQo is heavily dominated by his value range, which consists of premium hands like AA, KK, QQ, AK, and AQ. The pot odds do not justify calling, and continuing would leave us with severe reverse implied odds if we hit a King or a Queen on the flop. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

My hand is Queen of Hearts and Trey of Diamonds, which is a very weak starting hand with no connectivity or high-card potential. Given that I am on the button in a three-handed game, I am first to act pre-flop. There is no reason to risk chips with such a poor holding, so folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack.

With 86s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, this is a reasonable hand to steal with or play a raised pot against the Big Blind. Flash has a relatively short stack (about 35 BBs), so raising to 300 puts pressure on him. If he decides to 3-bet shove, I can comfortably fold, but if he calls, 86s has good playability.

I have a strong pocket pair (99) in the Big Blind. In a three-handed game, this is a premium hand, especially against a Small Blind open which typically has a wide range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative, define the hand, and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a favorable post-flop situation with a lower stack-to-pot ratio.

I opened with a suited connector (6h 8h) from the Small Blind, but Flash 3-bet to 1000 from the Big Blind. Flash has a relatively short stack (about 35 BBs to start the hand), meaning if I call, the SPR on the flop will be very low (~1.25). Suited connectors perform poorly in low SPR situations because they rely on implied odds and fold equity, neither of which I will have here against a strong 3-betting range. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

9c 5c is a weak suited gapper. Even from the button, it is generally too loose to open-raise, especially with the small blind sitting on a 38 BB stack that could easily 3-bet jam over a steal attempt. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

With Pro having folded on the button, it is down to me in the Small Blind and Flashlight in the Big Blind. I am holding Qh 7d, which is a marginal hand. While I could complete the small blind for an additional 50 chips, Flashlight has shown significant aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hands #58 and #84) and has a much larger stack. Playing a weak hand out of position against an aggressive big stack is a recipe for losing more chips post-flop. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 38 BB stack.

I am on the button with J9o. In a three-handed game, this hand is well within the standard opening range. My stack is around 38 big blinds, which is the shortest at the table, but still deep enough to play post-flop. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to try and take down the blinds or play the hand in position if called. Both opponents have shown aggression recently, so I must be prepared to fold if I face a significant 3-bet.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (6-2 suited). There is no incentive to defend my blind against a button raise with such a marginal holding, especially out of position. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I hold Pocket Kings (KK) in the Big Blind, which is the second best possible starting hand. Flash opened from the Button to 300. With a premium hand, I definitely want to 3-bet for value and to build the pot. Flash started the hand with around 3,825 chips, so a 3-bet to 1000 gives them room to shove worse pairs or suited connectors, while also setting up a low stack-to-pot ratio if they just call.

I opened to 300 from the button with J9o, which is a standard semi-loose open in three-handed play. However, Pro in the big blind has 3-bet to 1000. J9o is too weak to call a 3.3x 3-bet, even with position. Given my stack size of approximately 35 big blinds, I cannot afford to bleed chips by calling in these spots with hands that have poor playability and are likely dominated by a standard 3-betting range. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Q2 offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, and even in a late position, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such a weak holding, especially with a short-stacked opponent (Flash) at the table. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (8c 4d) after the Button folded. In a blind vs. blind scenario, 84o is far too weak to play, lacking both high card strength and post-flop playability. The most +EV decision is simply to fold and surrender the 50 chip small blind.
Holding Ace-Ten offsuit on the button, this is a clear opening raise in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to steal the blinds or build a pot with a strong top-pair type hand.

I am in the Small Blind with J9s, which is a strong hand for 3-handed play. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250 (2.5x). I need to call 200 more. My stack is roughly 35 big blinds, making this a clear call to see a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here because J9s plays well post-flop, and I want to keep the pot size controlled against the chip leader while out of position.

I am in the big blind with pocket tens, a strong starting hand. With a raise from the button and a call from the small blind, there is already 600 in the pot. Since I am out of position, 3-betting to 900 is the optimal move to take the initiative, isolate, or build the pot with a premium hand. This size is standard and effectively puts pressure on both opponents.

Facing a squeeze 3-bet from the Big Blind after the Small Blind flat-called my open, ATo is in a precarious spot. It is frequently dominated by the 3-bettor's value range, such as AK, AQ, AJ, or large pocket pairs. Without position or a suited hand to play post-flop easily, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

Flashlight has executed a large squeeze play from the Big Blind, raising to 900 over the Button's open and my call. While J9s is a decent hand 3-handed, I am out of position and would be calling 650 more, which represents about 20% of my remaining stack. With a resulting SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of approximately 1.2 on the flop, I would have almost no room to maneuver and would be frequently forced into a 'fit or fold' situation or a marginal commitment of my entire stack. Given my stack size of 30 BBs, it is better to fold now and preserve my chips for a more favorable spot where I can be the aggressor.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. My stack is relatively short at 3,325 chips (approx. 33 big blinds). Both Flashlight and Pro have significantly larger stacks. Raising here is the standard play to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) is appropriate given the previous hands' betting patterns and my stack size.

Holding Jack-Deuce offsuit (Jd-2h) from the Small Blind is a clear fold. The hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. With a raise in front of me from the button, calling would be negative expected value. Given the stack depths and the fact that the Big Blind has yet to act, folding is the most disciplined and correct strategic choice.

Facing a 3x open from the Button who is relatively short-stacked, Q5o is entirely too weak to defend with from the Big Blind. It's unsuited, poorly connected, and easily dominated. The mathematically correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine suited-ish (Qh 9s). In a three-handed game, this is a playable hand in late position. I have a healthy stack of 12,600, while Flash in the big blind is quite short-stacked at 3,375.
